title: Superworms could replace beetles for cleaning skeletal remains
gist: A PLoS One study finds superworm (Zophobas morio) larvae are a practical, lower-infestation-risk alternative to dermestid beetles for cleaning skeletal specimens, with an optimal ratio of 10–15 grams of larvae per gram of specimen minimizing cleaning time without damaging bone.

insights.claim: Superworm (Zophobas morio) larvae offer a practical alternative to dermestid beetles for cleaning skeletal specimens because they carry lower infestation risk, requiring only the larval stage rather than a full multi-stage colony.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Niche museum/forensic technique improvement; no market relevance.

insights.claim: Superworm larvae last 10–12 weeks versus five to seven weeks for beetles and don't pupate in crowded conditions, making colonies easier to manage and reducing escape risk.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Operational detail on containment advantages; specialist interest only.
insights.locator: For one thing, beetle colonies span all life stages

insights.claim: Dermestid beetles are the preferred skeletal-cleaning method because they remove soft tissue without damaging bone, but without strict containment they can escape and cause infestations that threaten museum collections.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Establishes the incumbent method's key drawback; contextual only.
insights.locator: Using dermestid beetles has become the preferred method

insights.claim: A ratio of 10 to 15 grams of larvae per gram of animal specimen proved most optimal, minimizing cleaning time while not damaging any bones.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: The actionable protocol parameter from the study; relevant only to museum/forensic practitioners.
insights.locator: The result: A ratio of 10 to 15 grams

insights.claim: Researchers cleaned varied specimens (including an alligator gar, Eurasian eagle-owl, and gray wolf) with commercial superworms, feeding the larvae fruit or vegetable peels because a flesh-only diet can prevent molting or hasten their death.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Method husbandry detail; no financial signal.
insights.locator: To find out, Rastekar et al. collected

insights.claim: The authors cautioned against immersing skeletons in even a 1 percent bleach solution because it can damage bone tissue, and noted varnish spray should be skipped in forensic settings since it interferes with CT analysis.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Procedural caveat for practitioners; specialist interest only.
insights.locator: While they did briefly immerse the skeletons
